critique.summary: The article discusses Intel Optane SSDs, highlighting their unique 3D XPoint technology which offers ultra-low latency, high durability, and consistent performance compared to traditional NAND-based SSDs. It covers the specifications, advantages, and intended use cases of Optane drives, as well as Intel's decision to discontinue further development of this technology in 2022.
mainPoints: Intel Optane SSDs use 3D XPoint technology co-developed by Intel and Micron, providing a hybrid of DRAM and NAND flash characteristics.
Optane drives offer significantly higher durability (measured in Drive Writes Per Day) and power-loss protection compared to NAND SSDs.
They deliver much lower latency (around 25 microseconds) and more consistent write performance than NAND-based SSDs.
Intel ceased innovation and flash storage activities related to Optane in July 2022 but continues to sell existing products, including new Optane Persistent Memory NV-DIMMs for Sapphire Rapids CPUs.
Optane drives are especially suited for high-write, low-latency environments such as databases, virtual desktop infrastructure, and caching solutions.
critique.truthfulness: The article's technical claims about Intel Optane SSDs, including their use of 3D XPoint technology, superior durability, low latency, and consistent write performance, align with information from reputable sources such as StorageReview.com and Intel's official specifications. The discontinuation of Optane development by Intel in 2022 is also confirmed by Intel's public announcements. However, the article lacks multiple cited sources and perspectives, relying heavily on the author's testing experience and secondary references. The performance and durability data are consistent with industry benchmarks, but some claims would benefit from direct citations to authoritative reviews or Intel documentation.
considerations: The article does not discuss the cost comparison in detail, which is a significant factor limiting Optane adoption.
It does not address the competitive landscape and advancements in NAND SSD technology beyond brief mentions.
Potential impacts of Intel's discontinuation of Optane development on the future availability and support are not deeply explored.
The article assumes familiarity with technical terms and does not provide simplified explanations for general readers.
Environmental and energy consumption aspects of Optane versus NAND SSDs are not considered.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on Intel Optane's unique technical advantages, particularly its low latency and endurance, especially for database and small random access workloads. However, high cost, limited commercial viability, and Intel's strategic decisions led to its discontinuation despite its technical merits.

comments.author: rkagerer
comments.text: My understanding is Optane is still unbeaten when it comes to latency. Has anyone examined its use as an OS volume, compared to today's leading SSD's?  I know the throughput won't be as high, but in my experience that's not as important to how responsive your machine feels as latency.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately highlights Optane's key strength in latency and raises a relevant question about practical usage compared to modern SSDs.

comments.author: dangoodmanUT
comments.text:
Optane was crazy good tech, it way just too expensive at the time for mass adoption, but the benefits were so good.

Looking at those charts, besides the DWPD it feels like normal NVMe has mostly caught up. I occassionally wonder where a gen 7/8(?) optane would be today if it caught on, it'd probably be nuts.
comments.analysis: Correctly identifies the main reason for Optane's limited adoption as cost despite strong technical performance.

comments.author: twotwotwo
comments.text:
One potential application I briefly had hope for was really good power loss protection in front of a conventional Flash SSD. You only need a little compared to the overall SSD capacity to be able to correctly report the write was persisted, and it's always running, so there's less of a 'will PLP work when we really need it?' question. (Maybe there's some use as a read cache too? Host RAM's probably better for that, though.) It's going to be rewritten lots of times, but it's supposed to be ready for that.

It seems like there's a very small window, commercially, for new persistent memories. Flash throughput scales really cost-efficiently, and a lot is already built around dealing with the tens-of-microseconds latencies (or worse--networked block storage!). Read latencies you can cache your way out of, and writers can either accept commit latency or play it a little fast and loose (count a replicated write as safe enough or...just not be safe). You have to improve on Flash by enough to make it worth the leap while remaining cheaper than other approaches to the same problem, and you have to be confident enough in pulling it off to invest a ton up front. Not easy!
comments.analysis: Provides a nuanced and technically informed perspective on Optane's niche applications and market challenges.

comments.author: hbogert
comments.text: It stands out, because it didn't sell. Which is weird because there were some pretty big pros about using them. The latency for updating 1 byte was crazy good. Some databases or journals for something like zfs really benefited from this.
comments.analysis: Accurately notes the paradox of Optane's technical strengths versus poor sales performance.

comments.author: pgwalsh
comments.text: Sure, they were expensive but they have great endurance and sustained read and write speeds. I use one in my car for camera recordings. I had gone through several other drives but this one has been going on 3 or 4 years now without issue. I have a couple more in use too. It's a shame this tech is going away because it's excellent.
comments.analysis: Provides a credible personal testimony supporting Optane's endurance and reliability.

comments.author: FpUser
comments.text: I feel sorry about the situation. From my perspective Optane was a godsend for databases. I was contemplating building a system. Could've been a pinnacle of vertical scalability for cheap.
comments.analysis: Expresses a personal viewpoint emphasizing Optane's value for database workloads.

comments.author: exmadscientist
comments.text:
Around the time of Optane's discontinuation, the rumor mill was saying that the real reason it got the axe was that it couldn't be shrunk any, so its costs would never go down. Does anyone know if that's true? I never heard anything solid, but it made a lot of sense given what we know about Optane's fab process.

And if no shrink was possible, is that because it was (a) possible but too hard; (b) known blocks to a die shrink; or (c) execs didn't want to pay to find out?
comments.analysis: Offers a plausible explanation about manufacturing limitations contributing to Optane's discontinuation, though based on rumors.

comments.author: amelius
comments.text:
For a good technical explanation at the physical level of a memory cell:

https://pcper.com/2017/06/how-3d-xpoint-phase-change-memory-...
comments.analysis: Provides a useful resource link for deeper technical understanding, adding value to the discussion.

comments.author: walterbell
comments.text:
Related: "High-bandwidth flash progress and future" (15 comments), https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=46700384

In an era of RAM shortages and quarterly price increases, Optane remains viable for swap and CPU/GPU cache.
comments.analysis: Correctly points out Optane's niche viability in specific memory-constrained scenarios.

comments.author: readitalready
comments.text:
These are absolute beasts for database servers, and definitely needs to make a comeback.

They suck for large sequential file access, but incredible for small random access: databases.
comments.analysis: Expresses strong positive opinion on Optane's database performance, consistent with technical consensus.

comments.author: ritcgab
comments.text: All those nice numbers are just beaten by the unit cost. And the ecosystem is a mess.
comments.analysis: Accurately highlights cost and ecosystem challenges as major barriers to Optane's success.

comments.author: rkagerer
comments.text:
Did anyone ever see retention issues like this guy reported on one of his older models?

https://goughlui.com/2024/07/28/tech-flashback-intel-optane-...
comments.analysis: Raises a valid concern about potential retention issues with older Optane models, supported by an external link.

comments.author: ashvardanian
comments.text: I don't have the inside scoop on Intel's current mess, but they definitely have a habit of killing off their coolest projects.
comments.analysis: Offers a critical but plausible opinion on Intel's corporate strategy affecting innovative projects.

comments.author: Havoc
comments.text: I've got a bunch of them in my homelab. They're good if applied to tasks that leverage their attributes.
comments.analysis: Shares practical experience confirming Optane's effectiveness when used appropriately.

comments.author: gigatexal
comments.text: I’m still sad they discontinued them. What’s the alternative now does anything come close?
comments.analysis: Expresses disappointment and questions the availability of comparable alternatives.

comments.author: ece
comments.text: Fabs are expensive and all, but maybe running a right-sized fab could have still been profitable at making optane for low-latency work that it was so good at. Even moreso with RAM prices as they are.
comments.analysis: Suggests a reasonable business strategy that might have preserved Optane production despite fab costs.

comments.author: myself248
comments.text: My kingdom for a MicroSD card with Optane inside. My dashcam wants it soooo badly.
comments.analysis: A humorous, emotional wishful comment without technical or factual contribution.

comments.author: bluedino
comments.analysis: Off-topic and adds no relevant information to the Optane discussion.

comments.author: gozzoo
comments.text: Maybe we can also mention the HP Memristor here.
comments.analysis: Mentions unrelated technology without context or relevance to the main discussion.
